refined_fr.refined_fr: Enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output to support validation against customer-defined energy efficiency targets.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the system's capability to measure/report energy metrics, and (2) the achievement of a performance target. The refined FR isolates the system's functional responsibility (instrumentation and data provision) from the acceptance criterion (target satisfaction), which is a verification gate, not a functional requirement. This keeps the FR solution-neutral by not prescribing how efficiency is achieved (e.g., via motor selection, load optimization, route planning, or operational procedures), only that the system provides the data needed to assess it.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that the NGHE solution collects energy consumption data (kWh or equivalent) and work output data (tons moved, distance, cycles) over the agreed evaluation period, and that reported energy_per_ton = energy_used / ton_moved can be computed and compared against E_target. Verification is independent of whether the target is met; it confirms the measurement infrastructure exists.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-003.1: Acquire and log energy consumption data from all power sources during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.2: Acquire and log work output metrics (e.g., mass moved, distance traveled, task cycles) during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.3: Compute and report the energy efficiency ratio (energy_per_ton) from logged data.
FR-NGHE-003.4: Provide traceability and timestamping of energy and work data to support evaluation period validation.

candidate_dps: DP1: Decompose energy consumption measurement into (a) instantaneous power acquisition from energy source, (b) continuous integration over evaluation period, and (c) independent work output quantification; correlate via post-processing to derive energy_per_ton ratio.

DP2: Embed energy metering at the point of energy conversion (e.g., motor input or hydraulic pump discharge) and work-output sensing (e.g., load cell, displacement sensor) as separate logical channels; synchronize timestamps and compute ratio in a dedicated energy-efficiency calculation module.

DP3: Establish a real-time energy-work correlation loop: acquire instantaneous power and work rate in parallel, compute running energy_per_ton continuously, and flag deviations from E_target threshold during operation; log all samples for post-evaluation audit.

DP4: Implement a modular measurement architecture with three independent logical subsystems—(i) energy acquisition and conditioning, (ii) work-output quantification, (iii) ratio computation and reporting—each with defined interfaces; allow substitution of measurement methods without affecting ratio logic.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP4: Implement a modular measurement architecture with three independent logical subsystems—(i) energy acquisition and conditioning, (ii) work-output quantification, (iii) ratio computation and reporting—each with defined interfaces; allow substitution of measurement methods without affecting ratio logic.

potential_sources: Timestamp synchronization between energy and work-output channels must be tightly coordinated; recommend explicit time-sync protocol at IM layer.
Energy conditioning (filtering, calibration) and work-output filtering must not introduce phase lag that distorts the ratio; recommend independent validation of signal integrity.
Ratio computation module depends on both upstream subsystems; recommend defensive input validation and error propagation strategy.
Evaluation period definition and data aggregation window must be consistently applied across all three subsystems; recommend centralized period-management module.
coupling_check.notes: DP4 achieves logical independence by separating concerns (energy, work, ratio) into distinct subsystems with well-defined interfaces. Coupling is limited to data flow and synchronization, not algorithmic or functional entanglement. DP1 and DP3 introduce tighter temporal coupling (continuous integration, real-time loops) that increases sensitivity to latency and jitter. DP2 is intermediate but less modular for future method substitution.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Energy source is measurable at a single point (e.g., electrical input, fuel flow, hydraulic pressure × flow). RISK: If energy is distributed across multiple sources (e.g., multiple motors, regenerative braking), measurement architecture must aggregate; recommend explicit source inventory in requirements.

ASSUMPTION: Work output is quantifiable via a single primary metric (e.g., mass moved, distance traveled, load lifted). RISK: If work definition is ambiguous or multi-dimensional, ratio may not be meaningful; recommend customer sign-off on work-output definition before IM design.

ASSUMPTION: Evaluation period is static and known in advance. RISK: If period is dynamic or event-driven, ratio computation logic becomes stateful and harder to validate; recommend clarification of period boundaries (e.g., shift, day, project).

ASSUMPTION: Measurement accuracy and resolution are sufficient to detect deviations from E_target. RISK: If sensor noise or calibration drift exceeds acceptable error margin, ratio may be unreliable; recommend sensor selection and validation plan at IM layer.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan does not prohibit installation of measurement hardware (e.g., load cells, power meters). RISK: If safety constraints restrict sensor placement or cabling, measurement architecture may be infeasible; recommend early coordination with safety team.

ASSUMPTION: Data logging and reporting do not introduce real-time performance constraints. RISK: If reporting must occur during operation (not post-evaluation), DP4 may require real-time subsystem; recommend clarification of reporting cadence.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) energy source(s) to be measured (electrical, fuel, hydraulic, etc.); (2) work-output metric and units (mass, distance, load, etc.); (3) evaluation period definition and boundaries (shift, day, project, continuous); (4) acceptable measurement uncertainty and sensor accuracy class; (5) reporting frequency (real-time, post-evaluation, both); (6) safety and operational constraints on sensor placement. With these clarifications, DP4 can be decomposed into three independent IM subsystems with minimal coupling and clear ownership boundaries. Recommend parallel IM design for energy acquisition, work-output sensing, and ratio computation to validate feasibility and cost.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates the complete modular measurement architecture with three independent logical subsystems: energy acquisition and conditioning, work-output quantification, and ratio computation and reporting. Provides substitutable measurement methods and unified ratio logic interface.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Exports computed energy ratio, work output, energy input, and measurement subsystem health status to external monitoring and logging systems.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives measurement configuration commands, method substitution directives, and calibration parameters for the three subsystems.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Signals measurement anomalies, sensor failures, or out-of-range ratio conditions that require safety intervention.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Provides optional remote access to measurement subsystem state, configuration, and diagnostic data for distributed teleoperation scenarios.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. NGHE Solution is the natural home for measurement architecture. All three subsystems (energy acquisition, work quantification, ratio computation) are core to NGHE functionality. Substitutable measurement methods align with NGHE's design philosophy. Minimal diffusion: only telemetry export and tasking input cross boundaries; internal subsystem logic remains fully contained.

Poor fit. Vehicle is a platform/host, not a measurement logic container. Placing measurement architecture here would scatter energy/work/ratio logic across vehicle subsystems (propulsion, sensors, control). High diffusion: energy acquisition would couple to vehicle power systems, work quantification to drivetrain, ratio logic to vehicle controller. Violates separation of concerns.

Very poor fit. Teleoperation is a command/control interface layer, not a measurement domain. Placing measurement subsystems here would entangle measurement logic with remote operation protocols. High diffusion: energy/work/ratio computation would leak into teleoperation state machines and network handlers. Violates single responsibility.

Poor fit. Safety Services is a reactive alert/monitoring layer, not a measurement computation layer. While SafetyAlert interface is used for anomaly signaling, the three measurement subsystems themselves do not belong in Safety Services. High diffusion: measurement logic would be scattered across safety rules and condition monitors. Violates cohesion.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: DP4 (modular measurement architecture with three independent subsystems and substitutable methods) is best contained within NGHE Solution because: (1) energy acquisition, work quantification, and ratio computation are intrinsic to NGHE's core function; (2) the three subsystems can be implemented as internal modules within NGHE with well-defined internal interfaces; (3) substitution of measurement methods is a design-time or configuration-time concern internal to NGHE; (4) external interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) cleanly separate measurement logic from platform, teleoperation, and safety concerns; (5) no other candidate module has sufficient functional affinity to contain all three subsystems without major diffusion.
containment_risks: Risk: If Vehicle platform imposes hard constraints on sensor placement or power availability, energy acquisition subsystem may require tight coupling to Vehicle hardware interfaces. Mitigation: Define energy acquisition DP with abstraction layer (e.g., sensor adapter interface) to decouple NGHE measurement logic from Vehicle-specific hardware.

Risk: If Teleoperation requires real-time access to raw measurement data (not just computed ratio), measurement subsystems may need to expose internal state beyond Telemetry interface. Mitigation: Extend Telemetry interface with diagnostic/debug telemetry stream; keep core ratio logic isolated.

Risk: If Safety Services must validate measurement subsystem health independently, there may be duplication of measurement logic or tight coupling between NGHE Solution and Safety Services. Mitigation: Define SafetyAlert as a unidirectional signal from NGHE to Safety Services; Safety Services consumes alerts but does not replicate measurement logic.

Risk: Substitution of measurement methods at runtime may require dynamic module loading or plugin architecture within NGHE Solution, adding complexity. Mitigation: Clarify whether substitution is design-time (configuration) or runtime (dynamic); design DP accordingly.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: STRONG. Acceptance criterion `energy_per_ton = energy_used / ton_moved ≤ E_target` is directly measurable and traceable to DP4's three subsystems: (i) energy acquisition quantifies `energy_used`, (ii) work-output quantification measures `ton_moved`, (iii) ratio computation produces the verifiable metric. NGHE Solution as IM boundary provides unified ownership for end-to-end measurement chain. Telemetry interface enables export of computed ratio and subsystem health for validation against customer targets over agreed evaluation period.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: EXCELLENT. Ownership is unambiguous: NGHE Solution owns all three measurement subsystems and their internal interfaces. External interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) cleanly delineate measurement logic from platform, teleoperation, and safety concerns. No functional ambiguity regarding which module is responsible for energy_per_ton computation or subsystem health monitoring.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP4 directly and sufficiently addresses FR. The modular architecture with three independent logical subsystems (energy acquisition, work-output quantification, ratio computation) and substitutable measurement methods provides the necessary logical structure to enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output. Separation of concerns allows independent validation of each subsystem and supports future method substitution without affecting ratio logic. DP is solution-complete for the stated functional requirement.

diffusion_risks: MODERATE: Energy acquisition subsystem may require tight coupling to Vehicle hardware interfaces (sensor placement, power availability). Mitigation: Implement sensor adapter abstraction layer within NGHE Solution to decouple measurement logic from Vehicle-specific hardware constraints.

LOW-MODERATE: If Teleoperation requires real-time access to raw measurement data beyond computed ratio, internal measurement subsystem state may leak beyond Telemetry interface. Mitigation: Extend Telemetry with optional diagnostic stream; enforce strict boundary that core ratio logic remains isolated within NGHE Solution.

LOW: Substitution of measurement methods at runtime may require dynamic module loading or plugin architecture, adding implementation complexity. Mitigation: Clarify substitution scope (design-time configuration vs. runtime dynamic loading) and design DP accordingly; if runtime substitution required, implement plugin interface within NGHE Solution to contain complexity.

LOW: Safety Services may attempt to replicate measurement validation logic independently, creating duplication and coupling. Mitigation: Enforce unidirectional SafetyAlert signal from NGHE to Safety Services; Safety Services consumes alerts but does not replicate measurement logic.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: EXCELLENT containment fit (95/5 score). NGHE Solution is the natural and only appropriate home for DP4. All three subsystems are intrinsically cohesive and belong together. Alternative candidates (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) exhibit high diffusion risk (65–80%) due to functional misalignment and violation of separation of concerns. Internal subsystem interfaces remain fully contained; external interfaces cleanly separate measurement domain from platform, control, and safety domains. No scattered responsibilities detected.

recommended_revisions: CLARIFY substitution scope: Explicitly state whether measurement method substitution is (a) design-time/configuration-time only, or (b) runtime dynamic. This determines whether DP4 requires plugin/adapter architecture within NGHE Solution.

DEFINE sensor adapter interface: Specify abstraction layer between energy acquisition subsystem and Vehicle hardware to mitigate coupling risk. Document expected sensor types, power requirements, and calibration parameters.

EXTEND Telemetry interface specification: Clarify which measurement subsystem internal states (raw sensor data, intermediate computations) are exported vs. which remain private. Distinguish between operational telemetry (computed ratio, health status) and diagnostic telemetry (raw data for troubleshooting).

FORMALIZE SafetyAlert contract: Document that SafetyAlert is unidirectional (NGHE → Safety Services), that Safety Services does not replicate measurement logic, and that measurement anomaly detection remains owned by NGHE Solution.

ADD acceptance test plan: Map acceptance criterion `energy_per_ton ≤ E_target` to specific test cases for each subsystem (energy acquisition accuracy, work-output quantification accuracy, ratio computation correctness) and integration tests for end-to-end measurement chain.

decision.justification: Gate results confirm FR4 is well-formed, DP4 is solution-complete, and IM (NGHE Solution) is the correct containment boundary. However, safety criticality is HIGH, and the gate identified four MODERATE-to-LOW diffusion risks that require architectural clarification before decomposition proceeds. The recommended revisions—particularly substitution scope, sensor adapter abstraction, Telemetry interface specification, and SafetyAlert contract—are architectural decisions that must be resolved at the current level to prevent downstream ambiguity and safety-critical coupling. Decomposing further without resolving these would propagate uncertainty into sub-FR design. ZIGZAG_DOWN to revise DP4 and IM boundaries, then re-gate before deriving sub-FRs.

revise.why: DP4 requires explicit architectural decisions on: (1) measurement method substitution scope (design-time vs. runtime), (2) sensor adapter abstraction layer interface and coupling strategy, (3) Telemetry export contract (operational vs. diagnostic data), (4) SafetyAlert unidirectionality and ownership boundary. These are not functional decompositions but rather design-domain clarifications that establish the logical and physical structure within which sub-FRs will operate. Revising DP4 to include these architectural constraints, and updating IM specification to document the adapter layer, sensor interface abstraction, and Telemetry/SafetyAlert contracts, will eliminate ambiguity and reduce diffusion risk from MODERATE to LOW before sub-FR derivation.

assumptions: Measurement method substitution is assumed to be design-time/configuration-time only; if runtime dynamic loading is required, DP4 must include plugin architecture specification.

Energy acquisition subsystem is assumed to interface with Vehicle hardware via standardized sensor types (e.g., power bus voltage/current, motor torque/speed); Vehicle-specific sensor placement and calibration are assumed to be Vehicle responsibility, not NGHE Solution responsibility.

Telemetry interface is assumed to export computed energy_per_ton ratio and subsystem health status; raw sensor data and intermediate computations are assumed to remain private unless explicitly required for diagnostics.
SafetyAlert is assumed to be unidirectional (NGHE Solution → Safety Services); Safety Services is assumed to consume alerts but not replicate measurement validation logic.
NGHE Solution is assumed to own end-to-end measurement chain; no other module (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) is assumed to duplicate or independently validate energy_per_ton computation.
open_risks: MODERATE: Energy acquisition subsystem coupling to Vehicle hardware. Mitigation required: Define sensor adapter abstraction layer with clear interface contract (sensor types, power requirements, calibration parameters, error handling).

MODERATE: Telemetry interface scope ambiguity. Mitigation required: Formalize export contract distinguishing operational telemetry (ratio, health) from diagnostic telemetry (raw data); document access control and data retention policies.

MODERATE: SafetyAlert contract clarity. Mitigation required: Formalize unidirectionality, document that Safety Services does not replicate measurement logic, and establish escalation path for measurement anomalies.

LOW-MODERATE: Runtime substitution of measurement methods. Mitigation required: Clarify substitution scope; if runtime dynamic loading is required, design plugin interface within NGHE Solution to contain complexity.

LOW: Safety Services may attempt to validate measurement independently, creating duplication. Mitigation required: Enforce architectural boundary via code review and interface contracts; document that measurement validation is NGHE Solution responsibility only.
evidence_needed: Revised DP4 specification with explicit architectural decisions on substitution scope, sensor adapter abstraction, Telemetry export contract, and SafetyAlert unidirectionality.
Sensor adapter interface specification: document expected sensor types, power requirements, calibration parameters, error handling, and Vehicle-to-NGHE Solution boundary.
Telemetry interface specification: clarify which measurement subsystem states are exported (operational vs. diagnostic), access control, data retention, and diagnostic stream design (if required).
SafetyAlert contract specification: formalize unidirectionality, document that Safety Services consumes alerts but does not replicate measurement logic, and establish escalation path for anomalies.
Acceptance test plan mapping energy_per_ton ≤ E_target to subsystem-level and integration test cases.
Re-gate DP4 and IM after revisions to confirm diffusion risks are mitigated to LOW and architectural boundaries are unambiguous before sub-FR derivation.
